class Solution {
    public int countKDifference(int[] nums, int k) {
        int count = 0;
        int n = nums.length;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            for (int j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
                if (Math.abs(nums[i] - nums[j]) == k) {
                    count++;
                }
            }
        }
        return count;
    }
}

class Solution {
    public int countKDifference(int[] nums, int k) {
        int[] freq = new int[101];
        for (int num : nums) {
            freq[num]++;
        }
        int count = 0;
        for (int x = 1; x <= 100; x++) {
            int y = x + k;
            if (y <= 100 && freq[y] > 0 && freq[x] > 0) {
                count += freq[x] * freq[y];
            }
        }
        return count;
    }
}

count += freq[x] * freq[x +k], without checking if they are positive. Because if x is present in the array, then freq[x] is positive. But if not, then it's zero, and the product is zero. So the code can be written without checking if freq[x] > 0 or freq[y] >0. Let me adjust that.

class Solution {
    public int countKDifference(int[] nums, int k) {
        int[] freq = new int[101];
        for (int num : nums) {
            freq[num]++;
        }
        int count = 0;
        for (int x = 1; x <= 100; x++) {
            int y = x + k;
            if (y <= 100) {
                count += freq[x] * freq[y];
            }
        }
        return count;
    }
}

solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public int countKDifference(int[] nums, int k) {
        int[] freq = new int[101];
        for (int num : nums) {
            freq[num]++;
        }
        int count = 0;
        for (int x = 1; x <= 100; x++) {
            int y = x + k;
            if (y <= 100) {
                count += freq[x] * freq[y];
            }
        }
        return count;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a frequency array to count occurrences of each number (1-100). For each number x, it checks if there exists a number y = x + k and multiplies their frequencies to count valid pairs. This approach is efficient with linear time complexity and constant space. It avoids checking all pairs directly, leveraging mathematical properties of absolute differences. The main weakness is the fixed-size frequency array, but this is acceptable given the problem constraints.
